questions.question_text: Under 20 CSR 2250-8.120, earnest money must be deposited no later than how many banking days after all parties sign?
distractors: 5 banking days
3 business days
15 calendar days
questions.explanation: Missouri regulation 20 CSR 2250-8.120 requires earnest money to be deposited no later than 10 banking days after the last signature or initials of all parties to the contract. The contract may specify a different timeline, but 10 banking days is the default.

questions.question_text: A purchase contract states earnest money must be deposited within 5 days of acceptance. The default rule allows 10 banking days. Which deadline controls?
distractors: The 10-banking-day regulation overrides
Whichever deadline is later applies
The broker decides which to follow
questions.explanation: While Missouri's default rule under 20 CSR 2250-8.120 allows 10 banking days, the contract may specify a different timeline. When the contract includes a specific deposit deadline, that contractual provision governs over the default regulation.

questions.question_text: All parties signed a contract on Friday, March 7. No special deposit terms were included. Using the 10-banking-day rule, when is the latest the earnest money can be deposited?
questions.correct_answer: By close of business on March 21
distractors: By close of business on March 17
By close of business on March 14
By close of business on March 24
questions.explanation: The 10-banking-day count begins the next banking day after the last signature (Monday, March 10) and excludes weekends and bank holidays. Counting 10 banking days from March 10 yields March 21 as the deposit deadline.

questions.question_text: Broker Harris receives a $5,000 earnest money check but waits 15 banking days to deposit it. No contract exception exists. What is the consequence?
questions.correct_answer: The broker violated the deposit deadline
distractors: No violation if the check eventually clears
Acceptable if the seller did not complain
Only a violation if the check bounced
questions.explanation: Without a contract provision specifying a different timeline, Missouri's 10-banking-day rule applies. Depositing 15 banking days after signing violates 20 CSR 2250-8.120 regardless of whether the check clears or either party objects.

questions.question_text: The 10-banking-day deposit clock for earnest money in Missouri begins after which event?
questions.correct_answer: Last signature or initials of all parties
distractors: The buyer delivers the check to the agent
The listing agent receives the signed offer
The seller verbally accepts the offer
questions.explanation: Under 20 CSR 2250-8.120, the 10-banking-day period starts after the last signature or initials of all parties to the contract. It is not triggered by delivery of the check, receipt by the listing agent, or verbal acceptance.

questions.question_text: What is the maximum amount of personal funds a Missouri broker may keep in an escrow account?
distractors: $500 for any purpose
$2,500 for service charges
No personal funds are ever permitted
questions.explanation: RSMo 339.105.1 allows brokers to maintain up to $1,000 of their own personal funds in the escrow account, specifically identified and deposited solely to cover bank service charges. This is the only exception to the commingling prohibition. Any amount above $1,000 constitutes commingling.

questions.question_text: Broker Wilson deposits $3,000 of personal funds into the sales escrow account to avoid overdraft fees. Is this permitted?
questions.correct_answer: No, the limit is $1,000 for service charges
distractors: Yes, if the funds are withdrawn promptly
Yes, to protect client funds from bouncing
No, but $2,500 would be acceptable
questions.explanation: Missouri permits brokers to maintain a maximum of $1,000 of personal funds in escrow solely for bank service charges. Depositing $3,000 exceeds this limit and constitutes commingling, regardless of the broker's intent to prevent overdrafts.

questions.question_text: A Missouri broker manages rental properties and handles sales transactions. How many escrow accounts are required?
questions.correct_answer: Separate accounts for PM and sales
distractors: One combined escrow account is sufficient
Three accounts: PM, sales, and operating
Only a sales escrow is required
questions.explanation: Missouri requires a separately designated property management escrow account for current rents and owner funds (20 CSR 2250-8.220(1)), maintained apart from the sales escrow account. Security deposits must be held intact in an escrow account separate from the PM account unless the owners agree otherwise in writing (8.220(2)). An operating account is not an escrow account, and one combined escrow account is not sufficient.

questions.question_text: Broker Taylor keeps rental security deposits in the same escrow account as sales earnest money. Is this a violation?
questions.correct_answer: Yes, PM and sales escrow must be separate
distractors: No, if accurate records distinguish the funds
No, one escrow account is sufficient
Only if the total exceeds $50,000
questions.explanation: Under 20 CSR 2250-8.220, property management funds must be held in a separately designated PM escrow account, and security deposits must be maintained intact in an escrow account separate from the PM account unless the owners agree otherwise in writing -- an exception that permits combining them with the PM account, not with the sales escrow. Record-keeping accuracy does not cure holding security deposits in the sales earnest-money account.

questions.question_text: A broker's escrow account has $800 of personal funds to cover monthly bank fees. The bank raises fees and the broker adds another $400. Is the broker in compliance?
questions.correct_answer: No, total personal funds now exceed $1,000
distractors: Yes, adjustments for fees are always allowed
Yes, if the broker notifies MREC within 30 days
No, personal funds can never be in escrow
questions.explanation: With $1,200 total personal funds now in the escrow account ($800 + $400), the broker exceeds Missouri's $1,000 limit for personal funds maintained for service charges. The broker must remove the excess to return to compliance.

questions.question_text: What information must each check or deposit in a Missouri broker's escrow account identify?
distractors: The broker's license number only
The listing agent's name
The MLS listing number
questions.explanation: Missouri requires that each check written from and each deposit made to the escrow account identify the related transaction. This ensures a clear audit trail linking every financial movement to a specific real estate transaction.

questions.question_text: MREC auditors arrive at a brokerage during regular business hours without prior notice to audit the escrow account. Can the broker refuse the audit?
distractors: Yes, 48 hours' written notice is required
Yes, unless MREC obtains a court order
Yes, if the account was audited within the past year
questions.explanation: MREC does not need advance notice to audit a broker's escrow account. Under RSMo 339.105.2, brokers must file written authorization allowing the commission to examine each escrow account, and under 20 CSR 2250-8.120(5) brokers must consent to examination and audit upon request. Records must be open to inspection at all times during regular business hours at the broker's usual place of business (RSMo 339.105.3), so maintaining accurate, current records at all times is essential.

questions.question_text: During an MREC audit, a broker cannot produce records showing which transactions correspond to specific escrow deposits. What is the likely finding?
questions.correct_answer: The broker failed to maintain adequate books
distractors: No violation if the account balance is correct
A minor bookkeeping error with no penalty
Acceptable if deposits match total contracts
questions.explanation: Missouri requires brokers to maintain books and records sufficient to determine the adequacy of the escrow account. If the broker cannot link specific deposits to transactions, the records are inadequate regardless of whether the account balance appears correct.

questions.question_text: What is the standard for record adequacy in a Missouri broker's escrow account?
questions.correct_answer: Books must show the account is adequate
distractors: Monthly bank statements are sufficient
Annual CPA review is the only requirement
Digital records are not accepted
questions.explanation: Missouri requires brokers to maintain books sufficient to determine the adequacy of the escrow account at any time. This goes beyond simple bank statements and requires transaction-level detail linking deposits, disbursements, and balances to specific deals.

questions.question_text: A broker maintains all escrow records electronically and shreds paper copies. An MREC auditor requests the records. Is the broker in compliance?
questions.correct_answer: Yes, if the electronic records are adequate
distractors: No, paper records are always required
No, originals must be kept for 7 years
Only if a backup exists offsite
questions.explanation: Missouri does not mandate paper records. Under 20 CSR 2250-8.160, brokers must retain true copies of books, records, and contracts for at least 3 years, sufficient to determine account adequacy. Electronic records that properly identify each transaction and allow auditors to verify account balances satisfy this standard.
